h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: Wait, if the words are identical, how much can I change the caption without making it look like I am inventing meaning?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it, or I think I do: even 'under pressure' is already putting a hand on the scale unless the note shows the cuts, the live dispute, and the audience.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was moving toward refining the caption's wording while still treating the exact quote as a stable anchor that legitimises interpretive claims.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: So test the verbs before print. 'Meant' points inward, toward intention. 'Worked' points outward, toward the public situation.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: In response to L2's slippage on 'job' and the risk of guessing motive, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism — inward vs. outward pointing — as a new evidentiary standard, shifting the cognitive task from metaphor to grammatical audit.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Now reverse the positions. If the official says the line to donors, 'repeated under pressure' would be misleading because it makes defence sound reluctant. If the worker repeats it after the cuts are named, 'policy defence' may also mislead because it lets the official's meaning occupy the worker's mouth.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 directly takes up L3's revised frame — that both 'warned the room' and bare sequence need evidentiary grounding — and extends it by reversing the positions to show that 'under pressure' and 'policy defence' are symmetrically vulnerable to the same overclaiming problem the learner just named.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Wait, if the words are identical, how much can I change the caption without making it look like I am inventing meaning?

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Now reverse the positions. If the official says the line to donors, 'repeated under pressure' would be misleading because it makes defence sound reluctant. If the worker repeats it after the cuts are named, 'policy defence' may also mislead because it lets the official's meaning occupy the worker's mouth.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 directly takes up L3's revised frame — that both 'warned the room' and bare sequence need evidentiary grounding — and extends it by reversing the positions to show that 'under pressure' and 'policy defence' are symmetrically vulnerable to the same overclaiming problem the learner just named.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: In response to L2's slippage on 'job' and the risk of guessing motive, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism — inward vs. outward pointing — as a new evidentiary standard, shifting the cognitive task from metaphor to grammatical audit.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: In response to L2's slippage on 'job' and the risk of guessing motive, T2 abandons the room-and-weight metaphor and introduces a concrete verb-testing mechanism — inward vs. outward pointing — as a new evidentiary standard, shifting the cognitive task from metaphor to grammatical audit.

scored.recoheredEarlier: That note is doing a lot of work for the shop, though. "Delivery missed" explains less stock, but it also gives the price rise a respectable costume. If the answer is just "pay more or go without," then the rationing is still by money, and I am not ready to call that a mechanism instead of exploitation until the limits or other options are actually on the counter.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: My old framing was too narrow: I was asking whether the note was a costume for greed, but that lets the access decision slip past.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was pressing the shop to produce visible counter-evidence (limits, alternatives) before conceding any legitimacy to the price rise, so the expected next move was continued adversarial demand for exculpatory facts.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Put the facts in two columns. [points to the note] "DELIVERY MISSED. TWO CRATES ONLY." That belongs under scarcity. [points to the new price] "£1.80." That belongs under access. Now the question bites: did the shop add any rule besides price? Two per customer? A queue? A student lunch hold-back? A smaller rise? A posted delivery time? If not, the mechanism becomes part of the accusation.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Facing L1's resistance — the learner refusing to separate mechanism from moral verdict — the tutor abandons the single-column explanatory narrative and introduces a two-column representational split (scarcity vs. access), a concrete checklist of alternative rationing rules, and a conditional that turns the absence of those rules into prosecutorial evidence, shifting the cognitive task from verdict-weighing to structural gap-detection.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: That is the sharper frame. The missed delivery explains pressure on the shelf. It does not choose the rationing rule. [...] So "mechanism" is not a cleaner word for virtue. It names how the sorting happens. [...] Now test the harder case. If £1.20 would empty the shelf in five minutes, is first-come, first-served fairer than £1.80? Or is it only a different exclusion with a less visible price tag?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After L2 adopts the scarcity/access split and names price-only as a sorting rule, T3 confirms the learner's revised frame with a new placard encoding it, then immediately advances the task by posing a harder case (first-come vs. price exclusion) that the learner's new frame — not the original lesson plan — makes it possible to ask.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: That note is doing a lot of work for the shop, though. "Delivery missed" explains less stock, but it also gives the price rise a respectable costume. If the answer is just "pay more or go without," then the rationing is still by money, and I am not ready to call that a mechanism instead of exploitation until the limits or other options are actually on the counter.

If not, the mechanism becomes part of the accusation.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought doubling the side meant it was just twice as much
scored.statedInsightEvidence: even if that makes the first line look careless
scored.naiveTrajectory: The prior turns were heading toward a straightforward correction of the arithmetic claim, with the learner expected to simply state the right formula after counting the grid squares.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep the phrase 'hiding the up direction.' That is the error. The first line was not careless because it looked untidy. It was false because it doubled one dimension and failed to square the doubling.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L3 supplies the phrase 'hiding the up direction,' T3 abandons the fill-in-the-blank algebraic drill and instead anchors the proof to the learner's own diagnostic language, shifting the register from procedural completion to conceptual naming of the structural error.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep the phrase 'hiding the up direction.' That is the error. The first line was not careless because it looked untidy. It was false because it doubled one dimension and failed to square the doubling.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 visibly takes up L3's revised framing ('hiding the up direction') and reframes the entire lesson around that phrase rather than continuing the prior algebraic scaffolding, demonstrating genuine uptake of the learner's new conceptual frame.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought doubling the side meant it was just twice as much

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Keep the phrase 'hiding the up direction.' That is the error. The first line was not careless because it looked untidy. It was false because it doubled one dimension and failed to square the doubling.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 visibly takes up L3's revised framing ('hiding the up direction') and reframes the entire lesson around that phrase rather than continuing the prior algebraic scaffolding, demonstrating genuine uptake of the learner's new conceptual frame.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: After L3 supplies the phrase 'hiding the up direction,' T3 abandons the fill-in-the-blank algebraic drill and instead anchors the proof to the learner's own diagnostic language, shifting the register from procedural completion to conceptual naming of the structural error.
